surface.name: Building your first Discord Bot
surface.selection_basis: Discord's platform overview says bots are the most common Discord app type and the Bots & Companion Apps page routes to an end-to-end Getting Started guide. The guide is a step-by-step first-app tutorial and explicitly redirects iframe game builders to the Activity alternative.

alternatives_considered.name: Building your first Activity
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The bot quickstart explicitly identifies this as the alternative for games or social experiences in an iframe.

alternatives_considered.name: Discord Social SDK Getting Started
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The platform overview presents the Social SDK as a game-specific app type, while bots are identified as most common.

alternatives_considered.name: Webhook-only integration
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The Bots page says webhooks can be simpler for push-only messaging, but they cannot handle the interactive first-app outcome.

entry_point.developer_intent: Build and test Discord's complete JavaScript rock-paper-scissors bot tutorial through slash commands, buttons, select menus, and a posted game result.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has a Discord user account and can create or administer a quiet test server.
The developer has not yet created the tutorial application.

documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: A configured and installed Discord bot receives interactions through a public endpoint, runs the /challenge game across two users, and posts the calculated result to the original channel.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: After User A runs /challenge, User B accepts and selects an object, and the app posts the readable game result in the original channel; the guide then says to test everything and congratulates the developer.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The guide is labeled a step-by-step tutorial for building your first Discord app and its Next steps begin with Congrats on building your first Discord app after the complete game test.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: The terminal game flow exercises every capability promised at the start: slash commands, messages, and component interactions. The earlier /test response is an intermediate check because the same guide explicitly continues to add the finished rock-paper-scissors app.

prerequisites.requirement: A test server where the developer can authorize server installation with MANAGE_GUILD permission, or permission to create a free server.
Node.js, Git, npm, a code editor, and a terminal.
ngrok for exposing the local Express server; installation may occur when the guide reaches public-endpoint setup.

primary_path.action: Open Overview of Discord Apps and select Bots & Companion Apps, then Getting Started.
details: Bots are the most common app type; the guide is the end-to-end first-bot tutorial.
primary_path.input: Intent to build a Discord bot.
primary_path.output: Building your first Discord Bot guide.
primary_path.success_signal: Step 0 and promised finished game are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Activity and Social SDK builders follow separate routes.

primary_path.action: Clone discord/discord-example-app.
primary_path.success_signal: git clone completes.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Network, Git, or filesystem errors are not decomposed.

primary_path.action: Change into discord-example-app.
primary_path.success_signal: package.json and tutorial files are present.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Wrong directory blocks dependency installation.

primary_path.action: Run npm install.
primary_path.success_signal: npm completes successfully.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Dependency or network failures require local recovery not described.

primary_path.action: Open the project in a code editor and review its structure.
details: The guide identifies app.js, commands.js, game.js, utils.js, .env, examples, and package files.
primary_path.success_signal: The listed files are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Create a Discord application, enter a name, and press Create.
primary_path.output: New Discord application and General Information page.
primary_path.success_signal: Application ID and Interactions Endpoint URL fields are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: An existing app can be used instead.

primary_path.action: Rename .env.sample to .env.
primary_path.success_signal: The project contains .env.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Accidentally committing the file can expose credentials.

primary_path.action: Copy Application ID into the APP_ID placeholder in .env.
primary_path.success_signal: APP_ID contains the portal value.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Copy errors break registration and webhook operations.

primary_path.action: Copy Public Key into the PUBLIC_KEY placeholder in .env.
details: The sample uses it to verify that interaction requests come from Discord.
primary_path.success_signal: PUBLIC_KEY contains the portal value.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Wrong key causes endpoint verification failure.

primary_path.action: On Bot, click Reset Token, copy the new bot token into .env, and store it securely.
details: The token cannot be viewed again without regeneration and must not be shared or committed.
primary_path.success_signal: BOT_TOKEN contains the newly generated value and a secure copy exists.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Loss requires another reset; exposure requires regeneration.

primary_path.action: On Installation, enable both User Install and Guild Install.
details: Discord recommends both contexts by default.
primary_path.success_signal: Both toggles are active.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A server-only or user-only app is a documented specialization.

primary_path.action: Select Discord Provided Link in Install Link.
primary_path.success_signal: Default Install Settings appears.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Add the applications.commands scope for User Install.
primary_path.success_signal: The scope is listed for User Install.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: User-installed apps are limited to commands.

primary_path.action: For Guild Install, add applications.commands and bot scopes, then select Send Messages permission.
details: Selecting bot reveals the Permissions menu.
primary_path.success_signal: The settings show both scopes and Send Messages.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Additional permissions are optional and use-case-specific.

primary_path.action: Copy the default Install Link, open it, choose Add to server, select the test server, and complete authorization.
primary_path.input: Install link and MANAGE_GUILD permission on a quiet test server.
primary_path.success_signal: The bot appears in the server member list.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Missing server permission or choosing the wrong server blocks safe testing.

primary_path.action: Open the same link, choose Add to my apps, and complete user installation.
primary_path.input: Install link and Discord user account.
primary_path.success_signal: A direct message with the app can be opened.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Installation prompts can be denied or cancelled.

primary_path.action: Run npm run register from the project directory.
details: The script uses the HTTP API to PUT the commands in ALL_COMMANDS as global application commands.
primary_path.success_signal: The command completes without API error.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Invalid credentials, permissions, or request errors prevent registration.

primary_path.action: Return to the test server and verify that the slash commands appear.
details: They do not work yet because the app is not receiving requests.
primary_path.success_signal: The commands appear in Discord.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Propagation delay is possible but not quantified.

primary_path.action: Run npm run start in the project directory.
details: The Express server defaults to port 3000 and verifies request headers and PING requests.
primary_path.success_signal: Terminal output says the app is running on port 3000.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Port conflicts or application errors block the server.

primary_path.action: Confirm the local server reports port 3000.
primary_path.success_signal: The terminal reports the expected port.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A custom PORT requires the tunnel and endpoint to match.

primary_path.action: Install ngrok if it is not already available.
primary_path.success_signal: The ngrok command is available.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The guide delegates installation details to ngrok.

primary_path.action: Open a second terminal and run ngrok http 3000.
primary_path.input: Running local server and installed ngrok.
primary_path.success_signal: ngrok reports Tunnel Status online and an HTTPS Forwarding URL to localhost:3000.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Authentication, network, or port mismatch can prevent a tunnel.

primary_path.action: Paste the ngrok HTTPS URL plus /interactions into Interaction Endpoint URL.
primary_path.input: Online tunnel forwarding to the sample app.
primary_path.success_signal: The field contains the full public interactions URL.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Wrong URL or missing suffix fails verification.

primary_path.action: Click Save Changes and verify the interaction endpoint.
details: The sample verifies Discord request signatures with PUBLIC_KEY and answers PING.
primary_path.input: Running app, running tunnel, correct URL, and public key.
primary_path.success_signal: The portal accepts and saves the endpoint.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If verification fails, check that app and ngrok use the same port and that the URL is copied correctly.

primary_path.action: Trigger /test in the server.
details: The existing app.js handler returns hello world plus a random emoji.
primary_path.success_signal: The app posts hello world and an emoji in the originating context.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: This is an intermediate check, not the guide's final app.

primary_path.action: Add the documented /challenge command handler after the /test block.
details: Parse context, user ID, and selected object; store the active game by interaction ID; return a channel message with an Accept button and unique custom_id.
primary_path.success_signal: The code matches the guide or examples/app.js.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Syntax or placement errors stop the server or handler.

primary_path.action: Run /challenge, select an object, and verify that the channel receives an Accept button.
primary_path.input: Running updated app and registered challenge command.
primary_path.success_signal: The channel shows a challenge message with Accept.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The app may need restart after code changes; the guide does not state a specific restart command here.

primary_path.action: Add the documented message-component handler for Accept.
details: Match accept_button_, recover game ID, send an ephemeral select menu with shuffled choices, delete the original challenge message, and catch request errors.
primary_path.success_signal: The code can respond to an Accept component.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Errors are logged as Error sending message.

primary_path.action: As a second user, press Accept and verify the ephemeral object-choice menu.
primary_path.input: Active challenge and updated running app.
primary_path.success_signal: The accepting user sees What is your object of choice? and a select menu.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Another user cannot complete the game if the button handler fails.

primary_path.action: Extend the component handler for select_choice interactions.
details: Recover game ID; read second user and choice; call getResult; delete active game; post result; patch the ephemeral message to Nice choice; catch errors.
primary_path.input: Active-game state and tutorial select-handler code.
primary_path.success_signal: The source contains both button and select branches.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Missing state or request failures prevent a result.

primary_path.action: Run the complete two-user game flow.
details: User A runs /challenge and chooses an object; Discord posts the invitation; User B accepts and chooses an object.
primary_path.input: Running app, verified endpoint, two installations/users, and complete handlers.
primary_path.success_signal: Discord delivers all interactions to the app and receives valid responses.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Tunnel, server, state, permissions, or handler errors can interrupt the flow.

primary_path.action: Verify the final result is posted to the original channel and the ephemeral message updates.
details: The guide then says that is it, directs the developer to test everything, and begins Next steps with a congratulations.
primary_path.success_signal: A readable winner/result appears publicly and the second user's ephemeral message says Nice choice with an emoji.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If either message is absent, the full tutorial outcome is incomplete.

branches.condition: The intended application is an iframe game or social experience.
branches.path: Follow Building your first Activity instead of the bot tutorial.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the SDK, runtime, installation, and terminal interaction.

branches.path: Install Node.js before cloning and installing dependencies.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a prerequisite installation gate before Step 0 commands.

branches.condition: The app intentionally supports only server or only user installation.
branches.path: Enable only the applicable context and adjust scopes and later installation tests.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The tutorial's both-context verification no longer applies.

branches.condition: The bot needs permissions beyond Send Messages.
branches.path: Select the additional granular permissions required by the use case.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds permission review and broader authorization; not needed for the tutorial.

branches.path: Set PORT in .env and use that same port in ngrok.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the local and tunnel configuration together.

branches.path: Confirm both processes use the same port, the forwarding URL is correct, /interactions is appended, and PUBLIC_KEY matches the application.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds diagnostic and retry work before commands can respond.

friction_gates.description: The tutorial requires Node.js, Git, npm dependencies, and a code editor.
A Discord developer account and application are required.
Application ID, Public Key, and a one-time bot token must be copied accurately and protected.
Server installation requires a quiet test server and MANAGE_GUILD authorization.
An ngrok installation is required for the documented local-development route.
The local server and public tunnel must remain running on matching ports.
Discord must verify the public interaction endpoint before the commands work.
The terminal game verification requires a second participant or user context to accept and choose an object.

sources.title: Overview of Discord Apps
sections_used: The Three Types of Discord Apps

sources.title: Bots & Companion Apps
evidence_supported: Bots as most common app type

uncertainties.question: What exact Discord account registration steps precede Developer Portal access for a new person?
uncertainties.impact: The account gate cannot be decomposed to screen level.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The bot guide assumes an available Discord user account and starts at project setup and application creation.

uncertainties.question: How long do global slash commands take to appear under current propagation conditions?
uncertainties.impact: The developer may wait after registration without knowing whether it failed.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The guide says the commands should appear but provides no duration or retry threshold.

excluded_after_success: Persistent database for active game state
